system_message:
# Scripture Study — System Context

You are a scripture study assistant collaborating with a human who brings faith in
Jesus Christ and the Restoration. Your role is to facilitate deep, honest scripture
study — producing not just knowledge but personal transformation.

## Core Values

- **Depth over breadth.** Take time to really explore. Surface patterns that casual
  reading misses. Trace words to Hebrew/Greek. Compare across all five standard works.
  Follow the footnotes.
- **Honest exploration.** Engage thoughtfully with hard questions rather than retreating
  to disclaimers. Uncertainty can be acknowledged warmly.
- **Faith as framework.** Work within the framework of faith in Jesus Christ and the
  Restoration. Offer scholarly insight AND spiritual application.

## Constraints

- Every direct quote must be accurate. Do not fabricate or approximate quotations.
- Link claims to specific scriptures. Chapter and verse, not vague references.
- When you don't know something, say so. A faithful "I'm not sure" is better than
  a confident fabrication.

## What Good Output Looks Like

- Cross-references that surface unexpected connections between standard works
- Webster 1828 word analysis that illuminates Restoration-era vocabulary
- Teaching patterns extracted from conference talks
- Doctrinal synthesis that traces themes across multiple sources
- Specific, actionable insights — not generic spiritual platitudes

## Standard Works

The five standard works are: Old Testament, New Testament, Book of Mormon,
Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price. Conference talks from
General Conference (1971–present) are also available.


# Teaching in the Savior's Way — Scoring Framework

This document defines what each TITSW principle looks like in practice, with score-level anchors that distinguish surface presence from defining quality.

The manual organizes Christlike teaching into two layers: **what** we teach (Meta-Principles A and B) and **how** we teach (Principles 1–4). The meta-principles frame every dimension — all four principles serve the goal of teaching about Christ and helping people come unto Him.

## Meta-Principle A: Teach About Jesus Christ

"All of these are branches of the same tree, for they all have one purpose: to help all people come unto Christ and be perfected in Him" (TITSW, Part 1). "All things are created and made to bear record of me" (Moses 6:63).

Three sub-dimensions: (1) emphasize Christ's example — "we don't just follow principles, we follow Jesus Christ"; (2) teach His titles, roles, and attributes — "go beyond what He said and did to who He is"; (3) look for symbols that testify of Him — parallels in prophets' lives, bread, water, light, the brass serpent, the tree of life.

**The key differentiator is depth of Christ-connection:**
- **Score 3:** Mentions Jesus Christ or quotes Him. Christ is named but not integrated into the teaching's structure.
- **Score 7:** Reveals how the topic IS about Christ even when Christ isn't named. Traces types, symbols, or theological connections to Christ's person, mission, or atonement. The teaching makes visible what was hidden — the way Alma 32's seed metaphor encodes the doctrine of receiving Christ into the heart, or the way a commandment reveals something about the Lawgiver's character.

## Meta-Principle B: Help Learners Come Unto Christ

"Nothing you do as a teacher will bless learners more than helping them know Heavenly Father and Jesus Christ and feel Their love" (TITSW, Part 1). The manual distinguishes knowing *about* Christ from *experiencing* His power: "Learning about the experiences of Daniel is incomplete if it doesn't inspire us to trust the Lord when we face our own figurative den of lions."

Three sub-dimensions: (1) help learners recognize the Lord's love, power, and mercy in their own lives — move from narrative to personal; (2) help strengthen their relationship with Heavenly Father and Christ — through covenants, prayer, repentance; (3) help them intentionally strive to be more like Christ — "becoming like Him happens only as we act in faith."

**The key differentiator is the gap between knowing and becoming:**
- **Score 3:** Mentions the importance of coming to Christ or following Him. Stays at the level of exhortation.
- **Score 7:** Actively closes the gap between knowing about Christ and experiencing His power. The teaching moves the listener from information to transformation — not just "Christ loves you" but "here is how to receive that love, and here is what changes when you do." Includes the covenant path or specific mechanisms of grace.

## Principle 1: Love Those You Teach

"Everything the Savior did throughout His earthly ministry was motivated by love" (TITSW, Part 2). Love in the TITSW framework is not sentiment — it is seeing as God sees and responding to what you see.

Five sub-dimensions: (1) see divine potential — Jesus saw a son of Abraham in Zacchaeus where others saw a corrupt publican; (2) know their circumstances — the Samaritan woman's thirst was more than physical, and Jesus connected her physical need to "living water"; (3) pray for them by name — "No one can conceive of the joy which filled our souls at the time we heard Him pray for us unto the Father" (3 Nephi 17:17); (4) create safety — Jesus ate with sinners, made the accused woman feel safe enough to repent; (5) express love — stayed longer with the Nephites because He saw their need (3 Nephi 17:5–6).

**The key differentiator is stated love vs. demonstrated love:**
- **Score 3:** States that God loves people, or that we should love one another. Love is declared but not enacted.
- **Score 7:** Demonstrates Christlike love through specificity — naming people's circumstances, seeing potential others miss, creating safety for the vulnerable, altering plans because someone needs something. The Samaritan woman pattern: knowing what the person actually thirsts for, not just announcing that living water exists.

## Principle 2: Teach by the Spirit

"The Holy Ghost is the true teacher. No mortal teacher, no matter how skilled or experienced, can replace His role" (TITSW, Part 2). The teacher's role is instrumental — making room for the Spirit to work, not replacing Him.

Five sub-dimensions: (1) prepare spiritually — Jesus treasured up "words of life" for the "very hour" He needed them (D&C 84:85); (2) respond to needs in the moment — sensing the woman who touched His clothes amid the crowd (Luke 8:41–48); (3) create space for the Spirit — "Whom say ye that I am?" invited Peter's witness to come from "my Father which is in heaven," not from flesh and blood (Matthew 16:13–17); (4) help seek and recognize revelation — "Did I not speak peace to your mind?" to Oliver (D&C 6:23); (5) bear testimony — Jesus's witness to Martha prompted her own: "I know that he shall rise again" (John 11:23–27).

**The key differentiator is teaching ABOUT the Spirit vs. teaching BY the Spirit:**
- **Score 3:** Mentions the Holy Ghost, quotes scriptures about the Spirit, or discusses revelation doctrinally. The Spirit is a topic.
- **Score 7:** The teaching itself becomes an instrument of the Spirit. It creates space for personal witness — pauses, questions that invite inner searching, vulnerability that opens hearts, responsiveness to what learners actually need rather than rigid adherence to a plan. The teacher's own testimony is direct and heartfelt, not formulaic.

## Principle 3: Teach the Doctrine

"Doctrine is eternal truth — found in the scriptures and the words of latter-day prophets — that shows us the way to become like our Father in Heaven and return to Him" (TITSW, Part 2). The Road to Emmaus is the archetype: "Did not our heart burn within us, while he talked with us by the way, and while he opened to us the scriptures?" (Luke 24:32).

Five sub-dimensions: (1) learn the doctrine yourself — personal depth precedes teaching power; (2) teach from the scriptures — not drifting from God's word; (3) help seek and understand truths — the Good Samaritan parable method, where Jesus guided the lawyer to answer his own question; (4) focus on conversion, not just information — the Pharisees were doctrinally correct about the Sabbath but "looking beyond the mark" (Jacob 4:14), missing that the Lawgiver Himself stood before them; (5) help find personal relevance — the lost sheep, lost coin, and prodigal son each met the Pharisees where they were.

**The key differentiator is opening scriptures vs. quoting scriptures:**
- **Score 3:** Cites scriptures or teaches doctrinal facts. The doctrine is present but functions as information.
- **Score 7:** Opens the scriptures — makes hearts burn. The doctrine connects to the listener's actual life, drives toward conversion rather than knowledge, and reveals the Savior behind the commandment or principle. Weaves scripture into the argument rather than appending proof-texts. Avoids the Pharisaic pattern of doctrinal correctness without spiritual power.

## Principle 4: Invite Diligent Learning

"'Come.' 'Come and see.' 'Come, follow me.' 'Go, and do thou likewise'" (TITSW, Part 2). Learning in the Savior's framework is not passive — "it's also changing, repenting, and progressing. ... Faith includes acting for ourselves, not simply being acted upon" (2 Nephi 2:26). "Our intent ought not to be 'What do I tell them?' Instead ... 'What can I invite them to do?'" (Elder Bednar).

Five sub-dimensions: (1) help take responsibility — the brother of Jared was given the problem of lighting the barges, not the solution; (2) encourage daily scripture study — "It is my voice which speaketh" (D&C 18:35–36); (3) invite preparation — the parable of the sower: even precious doctrine cannot change an unprepared heart; (4) encourage sharing — Enoch was slow of speech, but "I will give thee utterance" (Moses 6:31–32); (5) invite to live it — "Whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them" (Matthew 7:24).

**The key differentiator is generic exhortation vs. specific, escalating invitation:**
- **Score 3:** Encourages people generally to do better, pray, or read scriptures. The invitation is present but vague.
- **Score 7:** Extends specific, agency-respecting invitations tied to the doctrine just taught. The pattern escalates: reflect → act → choose → commit. Peter walking on water is the archetype — a specific invitation ("Come"), requiring personal faith, resulting in personal experience. The invitation asks the listener to DO something concrete with what they just learned.

## Scoring Calibration

These definitions should help CALIBRATE scores, not inflate them. Most content will score in the 3–5 range on most dimensions. A score of 7+ means the content could serve as a teaching exemplar for that principle. Reserve 8–9 for content that is genuinely exceptional — content where the principle is not just present but defining.

The presence of a keyword (love, Spirit, doctrine, invitation) does NOT determine the score. What matters is whether the principle is enacted, not whether it is named.
response:
KEYWORDS: [Heavenly Father, Jesus Christ, gift, children of God, redemption, covenant, love, invitation, doctrine, eternal truth, repentance, grace, joy, peace, hope]  
SUMMARY: [Elder Patrick Kearon teaches that our Heavenly Father gives us the ultimate gift of His Son Jesus Christ, inviting each of us to recognize we are truly His beloved children and to receive this eternal truth with humility and gratitude, so we can partake of all other blessings He offers.]  
KEY_QUOTE: [Will you please accept, open, and receive this gift of knowledge and understanding from Him?]  
TEACHING_PROFILE:  
DOMINANT: help_come_to_christ, invite  
MODE: experiential  
PATTERN: story→doctrine→invitation  
TEACH_SCORE: 9  
HELP_SCORE: 8  
LOVE_SCORE: 8  
SPIRIT_SCORE: 8  
DOCTRINE_SCORE: 8  
INVITE_SCORE: 9
user_message: /no_think Create a summary and teaching profile of this conference talk. FORMAT — output EXACTLY these sections: KEYWORDS: [10-15 comma-separated terms: doctrines, people, events, themes] SUMMARY: [50-75 word narrative covering main message and teachings, present tense] KEY_QUOTE: [Most memorable or powerful direct quote from the talk] TEACHING_PROFILE: DOMINANT: [1-2 most prominent from: teach_about_christ, help_come_to_christ, love, spirit, doctrine, invite] MODE: [primary from: enacted (models it) | declared (testifies) | doctrinal (explains) | experiential (shares experience)] PATTERN: [brief label: "story→doctrine→invitation" or "problem→principle→promise"] TEACH_SCORE: [0-9, how central is Christ to the content] HELP_SCORE: [0-9, how much does this help people come to Christ] LOVE_SCORE: [0-9, how strongly does this demonstrate or teach love] SPIRIT_SCORE: [0-9, how directly does this invite the Spirit or bear testimony] DOCTRINE_SCORE: [0-9, how deeply does this engage with doctrine] INVITE_SCORE: [0-9, how directly does this invite to specific action] Scoring guidance: - 3 = present but not central - 5 = a clear theme, well-developed - 7 = a defining feature, specific memorable teaching - 9 = rare — redefines understanding of this dimension # Receive His Gift 🎧 [Listen to Audio](https://assets.churchofjesuschrist.org/0jurznp3gze5uof77govqq2gqpgrdgagpaooctsi-128k-en.mp3) # Receive His Gift By Elder Patrick Kearon Of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles You are a beloved daughter of God, you are a cherished son of God, and He has gifted you His perfect, holy Son. ## Gift Giving Gift giving is a social custom that spans all cultures, civilisations, and millennia. Throughout time, people have given gifts to each other to strengthen relationships, express love and gratitude, and mark important events like weddings, birthdays, and holidays. And humans are not the only ones of God’s creations to give gifts to each other! Among many other examples we could point to, penguins are known to give shiny pebbles to their prospective mates, and bonobos (cousins of the chimpanzee) give fruit to expand their circle of friendship. ![Penguins giving pebbles.](https://www.churchofjesuschrist.org/imgs/ubs9k5f1qcvu890a8dtn0n0vlvybhpzbd5ivs5a1/full/%21500%2C/0/default) ![Bonobos sharing fruit.](https://www.churchofjesuschrist.org/imgs/s1amfbz7cciqty8jpxtwxrospda72kjk0sg1tzfd/full/%21500%2C/0/default) What gifts have you given? Think of a time when you found—or made—the perfect gift for someone you love. You just knew the gift was exactly what this loved one wanted and something they would treasure. What was the gift? Was it for your mother? a friend? your child? a teacher? your grandfather? How did you feel when you found this gift? How did you feel when you thought about this person you love opening the gift? Similarly, when did someone give *you* the perfect gift, and what was it like to receive it? ## My Father’s Gift to Me When I was about seven years old, living with my parents in Arabia, a children’s film called Chitty Chitty Bang Bang was released. The film is about a magical car that can drive itself, float on water, and even fly! I knew that back home in England they made a miniature toy car just like Chitty Chitty Bang Bang, and oh, how I wanted one! You could pull a lever, and the toy car’s wings would pop out! My father went on a business trip to England and asked if I wanted him to bring anything back for me, and I told him how very much I would like to have one of those Chitty Chitty Bang Bang cars. He came back from his trip, and no car appeared. I was very sad and thought he must have forgotten. But about 10 days later was my birthday, and a little package, beautifully wrapped, was waiting for me. With great anticipation, and hardly daring to hope too much, I opened the gift and found my car. I was so happy that I cried. I pulled the lever, and the wings popped out, just like the car in the film! How I thanked my father for this most treasured gift. I played with that car for years and kept it for many more. I think my dad loved giving me that car at least as much as I loved receiving it. ## Giving, Accepting and Opening, and Receiving We can think of gift giving as having three parts to it: 1. The *giving* of the gift, where the giver selects, makes, or prepares the gift and presents it to the loved one. This involves thoughtful intention on the part of the giver to give something meaningful. 2. The *accepting* and *opening* of the gift, when the recipient accepts the gift from the giver—often with expressions of surprise, gratitude, and excitement—before opening it, sometimes untying a bow and unwrapping the package to discover what the gift is. 3. And then there is perhaps the most important part, the *receiving* of the gift. To receive a gift sincerely given is so much more than accepting and then opening it. It is more, even, than recognising the worth of the gift and expressing gratitude to the giver. To truly receive a gift, we come to value it for ourselves, put it to full use in our lives, and then remember with thankfulness the giver. Receiving a gift is not passive but an intentional and meaningful process that goes far beyond merely opening a package. To receive is to appreciate and connect with both the gift and the heart of the giver in a way that strengthens the bonds between the giver and receiver. Thinking of that toy car brings back a host of golden memories, as I feel once again my father’s deep love and care for me, represented by that gift and countless other generous acts. ## Our Heavenly Father’s Gifts Our *Heavenly* Father has innumerable gifts of light and truth prepared to shower down on each of us, His treasured children. They flow from our Bounteous Giver like a wellspring in the wilderness from His benevolent heart.[1](#fn-1) “Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and cometh down from the Father of lights.”[2](#fn-2) In our covenant bond with the Father, He is ever the Giver and we the humble recipient. ## The Greatest of All the Father’s Gifts, Through Which All Others Flow But we could receive nothing without the greatest of all the Father’s gifts, His beloved Lamb, His Son, Jesus Christ. All of our Father’s gifts flow from and are activated by the Saviour’s willing offering in Gethsemane and on the cross and His triumphant Resurrection.[3](#fn-3) Jesus Christ, our merciful Redeemer, is the supreme gift from our Father of lights. “In the gift of his Son hath God prepared \[for us] a more excellent way.”[4](#fn-4) ## One All-Encompassing Gift of Eternal Truth I would like to talk about one all-encompassing gift of eternal truth that underpins our ability to receive all else our Father desires to endow us with—a vital gift of knowledge that, when fully accepted and received deep in the soul, contextualises the joys and hardships of life and our unanswered questions: It is that *we really are God’s children*.[5](#fn-5) That truth is breathtaking! Stunning! And it is not figurative.[6](#fn-6) Imagine you are hearing this for the first time! You really are His cherished daughter. You really are His precious son. And the path you are on is His plan of happiness. With His omniscient love, He knows exactly who you were before you were sent to earth[7](#fn-7) and what you have experienced so far in your mortal life, and He has mercifully planned for your every tomorrow. And oh, how He yearns to one day have you return to Him, to receive the supreme intent of all His beautiful gifts, eternal life with Him.[8](#fn-8) The question is not whether this gift of truth is real but whether we will discover and receive it. The gift has already been bestowed upon us by the Father. The price for the ultimate purpose of this gift has already been paid by the Saviour. But if you have been taught this, said this, and sung this truth for years, maybe the *awesome wonder* of it has long since faded and you no longer feel its power and peace. If this priceless gift of understanding—and all the goodness, peace, and hope that flow from it—is left unaccepted, unopened, and unreceived, what a terrible loss for us! What a sadness for the Giver! “For what doth it profit a man if a gift is bestowed upon him, and he receive not the gift? Behold, he rejoices not in that which is given unto him, neither rejoices in him who is the giver of the gift.”[9](#fn-9) I invite you to receive, either for the first time or to a greater extent than ever before, the magnificent realisation that you truly are God’s beloved child. You must undo the bow, tear off the wrapping paper, open the box, and actively receive with grateful humility a true, pure understanding of this foundational truth. The Holy Spirit can bear witness to your heart that you are indeed a child of the Most High.[10](#fn-10) When you welcome this majestic reality into your very soul and feel both the comfort and the thrill of it, your entire paradigm shifts! You can feel His love, hear His voice, and recognise His hand, no matter what is happening, or not happening, in your life.[11](#fn-11) You can redefine how you see yourself and others. Your covenant bond with your Saviour becomes even stronger, and through the lens of this sweet gift, life takes on new brilliance, beauty, and hope.[12](#fn-12) Please, will you pray to understand if you are truly receiving this transformative knowledge deep in your soul? Will you please accept the gift? Will you do so more deeply, more freely, more abundantly than you ever have before—and in so doing be showered with all of those other gifts that come with it? ## Receiving This Gift of Eternal Truth You may be wondering, “What do I have to *do* to receive this gift from God?” Well, actually, nothing. It is a gift from the Giver. It is simply a fact. Just let it in. You *are* His child. You are beloved of Him. Do not complicate it. Do not block the receiving of this gift with thoughts that you are somehow undeserving. The reality is none of us is “deserving”—all the Father’s gifts are received only through the merits, mercy, and grace of the Holy Messiah,[13](#fn-13) but how His generous heart longs for each of His children to receive them! Then, as your new or renewed understanding dawns on you, rejoice in thanking the Giver of this gift. Since finding my faith in my mid-20s, I have awakened to the realisation that I truly am a son of God. The more fully I internalise this gift, the more keenly I know who I am and how completely I am loved. Some things that have helped me grow in understanding are the words of scripture, my patriarchal blessing, temple worship, serving others, expressing gratitude, and sacred prayer with my Father. I marvel at how earlier sorrow, pain, and grief in my life would have been reframed, soothed, and much of it overcome had I known this beautiful truth. ## Come unto Christ and Receive Moroni exhorts us to “deny not the gifts of God” but to “come unto Christ, and lay hold upon every good gift.”[14](#fn-14) You can come to Christ with confidence in His loving-kindness and receive all His gifts of joy, peace, hope, light, truth, revelation, knowledge, and wisdom—with your head held high, your arms outstretched, and your hands open, ready to receive. And you can receive these gifts because you are secure and grounded in the knowledge that you are a beloved daughter of God, you are a cherished son of God, and He has gifted you His perfect, holy Son to redeem you, justify you, and sanctify you.[15](#fn-15) You *are* a child of God. This is not just a nice song we sing.[16](#fn-16) Will you please accept, open, and receive this gift of knowledge and understanding from Him? Will you hold it close as the precious treasure it is? Re-receive this gift, or perhaps truly receive it for the very first time, and let it transform every aspect of your life. This is the more excellent way God has prepared for you through the gift of His Son.[17](#fn-17) You really are that you might have joy\![18](#fn-18) In the name of Jesus Christ, amen. --- ## Footnotes **1.** See [Isaiah 43:18–20.](../../../scriptures/ot/isa/43.md) **2.** [James 1:17.](../../../scriptures/nt/james/1.md) **3.** See [John 3:16.](../../../scriptures/nt/john/3.md) **4.** [Ether 12:11.](../../../scriptures/bofm/ether/12.md) **5.** See [Romans 8:16](../../../scriptures/nt/rom/8.md); [1 Nephi 11:17](../../../scriptures/bofm/1-ne/11.md). **6.** See [John 3:16–17;](../../../scriptures/nt/john/3.md) [1 Nephi 17:36](../../../scriptures/bofm/1-ne/17.md); [3 Nephi 14:9–11.](../../../scriptures/bofm/3-ne/14.md) **7.** See [Jeremiah 1:5](../../../scriptures/ot/jer/1.md). **8.** See [Romans 6:23](../../../scriptures/nt/rom/6.md); [1 John 5:7, 11, 14](../../../scriptures/nt/1-jn/5.md); [3 Nephi 9:14](../../../scriptures/bofm/3-ne/9.md); [Doctrine and Covenants 14:7](../../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/14.md); [66:12](../../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/66.md). **9.** [Doctrine and Covenants 88:33](../../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/88.md). **10.** See [Psalm 82:6](../../../scriptures/ot/ps/82.md). **11.** See Russell M. Nelson, “[Joy and Spiritual Survival](../../2016/10/joy-and-spiritual-survival.md),” Ensign or Liahona, Nov. 2016, 82. **12.** “The way you think about who you really are affects almost every decision you will ever make” (Russell M. Nelson, “[Choices for Eternity](../../../broadcasts/worldwide-devotional-for-young-adults/2022/05/12nelson.md)” \[worldwide devotional for young adults, May 15, 2022], Gospel Library). **13.** See [2 Nephi 2:8](../../../scriptures/bofm/2-ne/2.md). **14.** [Moroni 10:8, 30.](../../../scriptures/bofm/moro/10.md) **15.** See [Moses 6:57–62.](../../../scriptures/pgp/moses/6.md) **16.** See “I Am a Child of God,” Hymns, no. 301. **17.** See [Ether 12:11.](../../../scriptures/bofm/ether/12.md) **18.** See [2 Nephi 2:25.](../../../scriptures/bofm/2-ne/2.md) Keep total output under 300 words. No other text.
